title: Where IMGs can work in Australia: geography and incentives
metaTitle: Where IMGs Can Work in Australia
metaDescription: How the Modified Monash Model, DPA and DWS decide where moratorium-restricted IMGs can bill Medicare, plus how to check any job's classification.
summaryMarkdown: If you're an IMG subject to the 10-year moratorium, *where* you work is often as important as the job itself, because location decides whether you can access Medicare. Australia maps every address to a remoteness category using the Modified Monash Model, from MM1 (major city) to MM7 (very remote). [[cite:mmm-scale]] That category, with the Distribution Priority Area (DPA) and District of Workforce Shortage (DWS) classifications, determines where a moratorium-restricted GP or specialist can bill Medicare. [[cite:dpa-imgs-medicare]] Rural and remote roles tend to come with more support and faster moratorium reduction through scaling. [[cite:scaling-more-remote]] The practical first step for any job is to check its classification on the Health Workforce Locator. [[cite:locator-purpose]]

sections.heading: The Modified Monash Model: MM1 to MM7
sections.bodyMarkdown:
The Modified Monash Model (MMM) defines whether a location is metropolitan, rural, remote or very remote, on a scale from MM1 (major city) to MM7 (very remote). [[cite:mmm-scale]] Areas classified MM2 to MM7 are considered regional, rural or remote, where it can be harder for patients to access a doctor. [[cite:mmm-mm2-7-regional]] The current classification is the 2023 MMM, which Services Australia implemented for Medicare and workforce programs on 13 April 2025. [[cite:mmm-2023-implemented]]

| Category | What it covers |
| --- | --- |
| MM1 | Major cities (metropolitan) |
| MM2 | Regional centres near a town of more than 50,000 people |
| MM3 | Large rural towns (15,000–50,000) |
| MM4 | Medium rural towns (5,000–15,000) |
| MM5 | Small rural towns |
| MM6 | Remote communities |
| MM7 | Very remote communities |

The MMM is descriptive — it tells you how remote a location is. On its own it doesn't grant or remove Medicare access, but it feeds directly into the DPA and DWS classifications that do. [[cite:dpa-method-mmm]]

sections.heading: DPA, DWS and where you can bill Medicare
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Under Section 19AB of the *Health Insurance Act 1973*, IMGs and foreign graduates of accredited medical schools can only access Medicare in areas with a workforce shortage. [[cite:dpa-imgs-medicare]] Which classification applies depends on whether you're a GP or a non-GP specialist:

- **GPs** must work in a **Distribution Priority Area (DPA)** — a location identified as short of GP services. [[cite:dpa-definition]]
- **Non-GP specialists** may need to work in a **District of Workforce Shortage (DWS)** — an area with poor access to that specialty, measured by the specialist-to-population ratio in an SA3 region. [[cite:dws-definition]]

The MMM is built into the DPA rules: areas classified MM2 to MM7 are **automatically** a DPA, while MM1 inner-metropolitan areas are not, and the Northern Territory and Tasmania are classified as DPA. [[cite:dpa-mm2-7-automatic]] So in practice, almost any genuinely regional, rural or remote GP role sits in a DPA. Because Medicare eligibility turns on these classifications, see the [Medicare page](/medicare) for Section 19AB and provider numbers.

sections.heading: Faster moratorium reduction in rural and remote areas
sections.bodyMarkdown:
From the date of registration, IMGs and foreign graduates must work at least 10 years in a DPA (GPs) or DWS (non-GP specialists) to provide Medicare-rebatable services — the 10-year moratorium. It ends after 10 years for permanent residents and citizens, and continues while you remain a temporary resident. [[cite:moratorium-basics]]

You can reduce the time spent in these areas through **scaling**: working in eligible regional and remote locations earns 'scaling credits', and the more remote the area, the more credits you earn. [[cite:scaling-more-remote]] Enough credits can earn a class exemption for the rest of your moratorium — though the moratorium is always a minimum of 10 years. [[cite:scaling-minimum]] Credits are applied automatically once your billing data reaches the threshold, based on where you do most of your Medicare claiming. [[cite:scaling-automatic]] This is why a remote posting can be attractive: alongside incentives attached to MM locations, it can shorten the path out of location restrictions.

sections.heading: 'Area of need' positions
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Separately from Medicare classifications, states and territories can declare a position an **'area of need'**. This is a *registration* concept, not a Medicare one, and it's determined by the relevant state or territory health authority — the AMC, the colleges and the Medical Board have no role in deciding which areas are an area of need. [[cite:area-of-need-declared]]

For specialist IMGs on the area-of-need stream, the process is: secure a position the state/territory has identified as an area of need; apply to the relevant college for an area-of-need suitability assessment; [[cite:area-of-need-process]] and if assessed as suitable, apply to the Medical Board for limited registration for area of need (or provisional registration if you're also eligible for the competent authority pathway). [[cite:area-of-need-limited-reg]] This is tied to the specific position. See the [Specialist pathway](/pathways/specialist) for how the streams fit together.

sections.heading: How to check a job's classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Before you accept a role, check the classification of its exact location using the Australian Government's **Health Workforce Locator**, which shows an area's classifications and any workforce incentives. [[cite:locator-purpose]] For a single address it shows the MMM category (MM1–MM7), whether it's a DPA, whether it's a DWS (searchable by specialty), and the ASGS Remoteness Area that scaling is based on. [[cite:locator-classifications]]

**A practical checklist:**

1. Enter the practice's exact street address — classifications can change street by street, so use the workplace address, not the nearest town.
2. If you're a GP, confirm the catchment is a **DPA**; if a non-GP specialist, confirm the area is a **DWS** for your specialty.
3. Note the MMM category — more remote generally means stronger incentives and faster scaling.
4. Record the result with the date, because the DPA and MMM were last updated in March 2025 and are reviewed annually. [[cite:dpa-locator-updated]]

If a classification later changes, doctors who already hold an exemption can generally continue at their location while they keep meeting its conditions. [[cite:dpa-change-protection]]

claims.text: The Modified Monash Model measures remoteness on a scale from MM1 (a major city) to MM7 (very remote).
Areas classified MM2 to MM7 are considered regional, rural or remote, where patients can find it harder to access a doctor.
The 2023 Modified Monash Model is the current classification, implemented by Services Australia for Medicare and workforce programs on 13 April 2025.
The Distribution Priority Area methodology considers Medicare billings and the remoteness of an area as classified by the Modified Monash Model.
Under Section 19AB, IMGs and foreign graduates of accredited medical schools can only access Medicare in areas with a workforce shortage.
The DPA classification identifies locations short of GP services, and IMG GPs must work in a DPA to be eligible to access Medicare.

A District of Workforce Shortage is an area with poor access to specialists, measured by the specialist-to-population ratio in an SA3 area; overseas-trained specialists subject to Section 19AB may need to work in a DWS to access Medicare.
Areas classified MM2 to MM7 are automatically a DPA, MM1 inner-metropolitan areas are not, and the Northern Territory and Tasmania are classified as DPA.

From registration, IMGs must work at least 10 years in a DPA (GPs) or DWS (non-GP specialists) to bill Medicare; this ends after 10 years for permanent residents/citizens but continues for temporary residents.
IMGs can reduce their moratorium through scaling, earning more credits the more remote the eligible area they work in.
The moratorium is always a minimum of 10 years, but enough scaling credits can earn a class exemption for the remainder.
Medicare applies scaling credits automatically once billing data reaches the threshold, based on where the doctor does most of their claiming.
An 'area of need' is determined by the relevant state or territory health authority; the AMC, colleges and Medical Board have no role in declaring it.
A specialist IMG on the area-of-need stream must secure an area-of-need position and apply to the relevant college for a suitability assessment.
Specialist IMGs on the area-of-need stream apply for limited registration for area of need (or provisional registration if also eligible for the competent authority pathway).
The Health Workforce Locator lets you enter any Australian address to check its classification and available workforce incentives.
The Health Workforce Locator shows an area's DPA, DWS, MMM and ASGS Remoteness Area classifications, and specialists can search by specialty.
The DPA and MMM were last updated in March 2025 and are reflected on the Health Workforce Locator, with DPA reviewed annually.
If a catchment loses DPA status, doctors who hold an existing Section 19AB exemption can generally continue at their location while meeting its conditions.

faq.question: What does MM1 to MM7 mean?
faq.answerMarkdown: MM stands for Modified Monash — a remoteness scale from MM1 (a major city) to MM7 (very remote). MM2 covers regional centres, MM3–MM5 rural towns, and MM6–MM7 remote and very remote communities. The scale feeds into the DPA and DWS rules that decide Medicare access.

faq.question: How do I find out the classification of a job's location?
faq.answerMarkdown: Use the Health Workforce Locator. Enter the practice's exact street address and it shows the MMM category, whether the location is a DPA or DWS, and its remoteness area, plus incentives. Use the actual workplace address, not the nearest town.

faq.question: I'm an IMG GP — where can I bill Medicare?
faq.answerMarkdown: While subject to the moratorium, you generally need to work in a Distribution Priority Area (DPA). Almost all MM2–MM7 areas are automatically a DPA, and the NT and Tasmania are classified as DPA, so most regional, rural and remote GP roles qualify. Confirm the specific catchment on the Health Workforce Locator.

faq.question: What's the difference between DPA and DWS?
faq.answerMarkdown: Both identify shortage areas, for different doctors. DPA applies to GPs (catchments short of GP services); DWS applies to non-GP specialists (specialist-to-population ratio in an SA3 area). They're calculated differently.

faq.question: Does working somewhere more remote shorten my moratorium?
faq.answerMarkdown: It can. The moratorium is always a minimum of 10 years, but scaling lets you reduce time in restricted areas — the more remote the location, the more credits you earn, and enough credits can lead to a class exemption. Credits apply automatically once billing reaches the threshold.

faq.question: What is an 'area of need' position and who decides it?
faq.answerMarkdown: A position a state or territory health authority declares to address a workforce gap — the state/territory decides, not the AMC, colleges or Medical Board. A specialist IMG secures the role, gets a college suitability assessment, then applies for limited registration for area of need.

faq.question: Is an 'area of need' the same as a DPA or DWS?
faq.answerMarkdown: No. 'Area of need' is a registration concept set by states/territories and tied to a specific job; DPA and DWS are federal Medicare classifications that decide where you can bill. Check the registration requirement with the employer and the Medicare classification on the Health Workforce Locator.

faq.question: What if a location loses its DPA status?
faq.answerMarkdown: DPA classifications are reviewed annually and can change. If a catchment loses DPA status, doctors who already hold a Section 19AB exemption can usually keep practising there while they meet its conditions. Record the classification, with the date, when you accept a role.
